result.markdown: --- title: "QC & Filtering" subtitle: "Assess and remove low-quality cells before downstream analysis" categories: [scRNA-seq, Seurat, QC] --- scRNA-seq · Chapter 01 ## Overview Raw single-cell RNA-seq data invariably contains low-quality cells — damaged cells with compromised membranes, empty droplets, or multiplets (two or more cells captured in one droplet). Retaining these artefacts distorts downstream clustering and differential expression results. **This chapter covers:** 1. Loading Cell Ranger output into a Seurat object 2. Computing per-cell QC metrics 3. Selecting filtering thresholds from QC distributions 4. Doublet detection with DoubletFinder 5. Saving the filtered object for normalization --- ## Setup ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(Seurat) library(ggplot2) library(patchwork) library(dplyr) ``` ::: --- ## Load Data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # From Cell Ranger filtered output directory counts <- Read10X( data.dir = "data/raw/pbmc3k/filtered_feature_bc_matrix/" ) # --- OR from .h5 file --- counts <- Read10X_h5("data/raw/pbmc3k/filtered_feature_bc_matrix.h5") seu <- CreateSeuratObject( counts = counts, project = "PBMC3k", min.cells = 3, # retain genes detected in ≥ 3 cells min.features = 200 # retain cells with ≥ 200 genes detected ) seu ``` ::: ::: {.callout-note} `min.cells = 3` and `min.features = 200` are deliberately loose. Stricter thresholds are applied after inspecting QC metric distributions. ::: --- ## Compute QC Metrics ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Mitochondrial gene percentage — high % indicates damaged/dying cells seu[["pct_mt"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(seu, pattern = "^MT-") # Ribosomal gene percentage — context-dependent, informative to track seu[["pct_rb"]] <- PercentageFeatureSet(seu, pattern = "^RP[SL]") # Preview metadata head(seu@meta.data, 5) ``` ::: --- ## Visualise QC Distributions ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} VlnPlot( seu, features = c("nFeature_RNA", "nCount_RNA", "pct_mt"), ncol = 3, pt.size = 0.05, alpha = 0.3 ) & theme(axis.title.x = element_blank()) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p1 <- FeatureScatter(seu, "nCount_RNA", "nFeature_RNA") + geom_hline(yintercept = c(200, 6000), linetype = "dashed", color = "#FF6B6B", linewidth = 0.6) p2 <- FeatureScatter(seu, "nCount_RNA", "pct_mt") + geom_hline(yintercept = 20, linetype = "dashed", color = "#FF6B6B", linewidth = 0.6) p1 | p2 ``` ::: --- ## Apply Filters ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} seu <- subset( seu, subset = nFeature_RNA > 200 & nFeature_RNA < 6000 & pct_mt < 20 ) cat("Cells retained:", ncol(seu), "\n") ``` ::: ::: {.callout-warning} ## Threshold Selection is Dataset-Specific The values above are appropriate for PBMC datasets. Adjust based on: - **Cell type composition** — neurons and hepatocytes naturally express more genes - **Sequencing depth** — deeper libraries shift the `nCount_RNA` distribution - **Tissue of origin** — immune cells have lower ambient RNA than solid tissues ::: --- ## Doublet Detection Doublets are droplets containing two cells that appear as one. Even after ambient RNA filtering, doublets can form distinct spurious clusters. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # DoubletFinder requires a preliminary normalization + PCA # (these will be done properly in Chapter 02; here they are run solely for doublet detection) seu <- NormalizeData(seu) seu <- FindVariableFeatures(seu) seu <- ScaleData(seu) seu <- RunPCA(seu, npcs = 20) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(DoubletFinder) # Expected doublet rate: ~0.8% per 1,000 cells recovered (10x estimate) nExp <- round(0.075 * ncol(seu)) seu <- doubletFinder( seu, PCs = 1:20, pN = 0.25, pK = 0.09, # pK should be optimised per sample — see ?paramSweep nExp = nExp, reuse.pANN = FALSE ) # Rename the classification column for clarity seu$doublet_class <- seu[[grep("DF.classifications", colnames(seu@meta.data), value = TRUE)]] # Remove doublets seu <- subset(seu, subset = doublet_class == "Singlet") cat("Singlets retained:", ncol(seu), "\n") ``` ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## Optimising pK Run `paramSweep()` followed by `summarizeSweep()` and `find.pK()` to determine the optimal `pK` value for your dataset. This adds ~5–10 minutes but meaningfully improves doublet detection accuracy. ::: --- ## Summary Statistics ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} data.frame( Step = c("After CreateSeuratObject", "After QC filter", "After doublet removal"), Cells = c(ncol_raw, ncol_filtered, ncol(seu)), Median_nGene = c( median(raw_meta$nFeature_RNA), median(filt_meta$nFeature_RNA), median(seu$nFeature_RNA) ) ) ``` ::: --- ## Save Checkpoint ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} saveRDS(seu, "data/processed/seu_01_filtered.rds") ``` ::: --- ## Session Info ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sessionInfo() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` R version 4.5.2 (2025-10-31) Platform: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu Running under: Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS Matrix products: default BLAS: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/blas/libblas.so.3.12.0
